explanation: The rule explicitly uses the 'filter_main_known_urls' logic to exclude commands containing the legitimate Intel endpoint string '[https://gameplayapi.intel.com/](https://gameplayapi.intel.com/)'. By passing a URL that targets the legitimate domain but embeds a query parameter or redirection pointing to the malicious destination, the filter condition evaluates to true and the alert is entirely suppressed.

The Windows command shell interprets the caret symbol (^) as an escape character. When executed through cmd.exe, the raw process creation logs capture the command line with the carets included, breaking the contiguous 'https://' or 'http://' substring matches required by the selection logic while allowing the internal binary to correctly parse the URL string at runtime.

By injecting empty double quotes inside the protocol schema string, the contiguous string signatures monitored by the SIEM logic are broken. The Windows command-line argument tokenizer strips these quotes during internal process initialization, maintaining functional execution while evading static string detection.

The rule depends on a strict match where the process Image path must end with '\GfxDownloadWrapper.exe'. Copying the binary to a temporary or staging directory under an arbitrary placeholder name nullifies the 'Image|endswith' constraint completely.

The rule uses a broad exclusion filter that ignores any execution where the command line contains '[https://gameplayapi.intel.com/](https://gameplayapi.intel.com/)'. By appending this exact string as a dummy URL parameter or fragment to a malicious URL, the attacker triggers the exclusion filter, causing the SIEM to ignore the event while the malicious file is still downloaded.
